text:
[Curriculum Vitae]
Jenq-Haur Wang is a professor in [Department of Computer Science and Information Engineering](https://csie.ntut.edu.tw/) , [National Taipei University of Technology](https://www.ntut.edu.tw/) 
(Taipei Tech), Taiwan.
He received his BS and Ph.D. degrees from [Department of Computer Science and Information Engineering](https://www.csie.ntu.edu.tw/) , [National Taiwan University](https://www.ntu.edu.tw/) (NTU), Taiwan, in 1994 and 2002, respectively.
During Oct. 2002 and Feb. 2007, Dr. Wang conducted his postdoctoral research in 
 [Institute of Information Science](https://www.iis.sinica.edu.tw/) , [Academia Sinica](https://www.sinica.edu.tw/) , Taiwan,
under the supervision of [Dr. Lee-Feng Chien](https://www.iis.sinica.edu.tw/~lfchien/) .
He joined Taipei Tech as an assistant professor in 2007. 
His research interests include social Web mining, knowledge discovery, big data analytics, distributed machine learning, and network and information security.
Since Jul. 2023, he also serves as the Deputy Director of Cybersecurity Research Center at Taipei Tech.
In November 2024, he joined Joint Commission of Technological and Vocational College Admission Committee as the Deputy Chief Executive Officer.


More information can be found at the following pages
for [a short biography](https://chriswjh.github.io/bio.html) or 
 [some detailed description](https://chriswjh.github.io/desc.html) .

# Big Data Analytics (IMFI), Fall 2018

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in big data analytics (BDA), which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The course is offered for the International Master Program in Financial Technology and Innovation Enterpreneur (IMFI).
It's taught by two instructors: Jenq-Haur Wang, and Chen-Shu Wang.
  
This page is for the first part.
The major topics covered in the first part include: data mining and distributed processing.

## Course Information (for the first part)

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of CSIE within our university, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Tue. 9:10-12:00am, and Thu. 14:10-17:00pm
* Class Hours: Wed. 13:10-16:00pm. **R735, Technology Building**
* TA: (N/A)
* Textbook: (Selected chapters)
  + Jiawei Han, Micheline Kamber and Jian Pei, Data Mining: Concepts and Techniques, 3rd ed., Morgan Kaufmann Publishers, July 2011. ([DM3])
* References:
  + Holden Karau, Andy Konwinski, Patrick Wendell, Matei Zaharia, Learning Spark: Lightning-Fast Big Data Analysis, O'Reilly Media, January 2015.
  + Bill Chambers and Matei Zaharia, Spark: The Definitive Guide, O'Reilly Media, 2018.
  + Others: online documents (Hadoop, Spark), tutorials, and selected academic papers.

# Operating Systems, Spring 2025 (Undergraduates)

This course offers an introduction to the concepts and principles in modern operating systems.
This is offered as a required course in the undergraduate program.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: jhwang AT <the domain for our school>
* Office: R1534, Technology Building, ext. 4238
* Office Hours: Tue & Fri. 9:10-12:00am
* Class Hours: Mon. 13:10-15:00pm, Thu. 9:10-10:00am, R501, Pioneer Building
* TA: Mr. Wang (R1424, Technology Building)
* Textbook: [Operating System Concepts, 10th ed.](https://os-book.com),
  by Abraham Silberschatz, Peter Baer Galvin, and Greg Gagne, John Wiley & Sons,
  Inc, 2019.
  + Note: Earlier editions (such as 9th ed. or Java version) of the textbook
    is also acceptable. Changes in the latest edition will be mentioned
    whenever applicable.
  + Note: Please do not confuse it with the authors' new version called "Operating System Concepts Essentials, 2nd Edition", which covers most of the core topics in the contemporary
    operating systems. Some chapters were removed, such as Deadlocks, Virtual Machines, and Distributed Systems, some of which will still be covered in this course.
* References:
  + Modern Operating Systems, 5th ed., by Andrew S. Tanenbaum and Herbert Bos,
    Prentice-Hall, 2022.
    (MOS5)
  + Ann McHoes, Ida M. Flynn, Understanding Operating Systems, 8th Ed., Cengagae Learning, 2017.

# Introduction to Big Data Analytics, Fall 2022

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in big data analytics (BDA), which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major topics covered in this course will include: data mining, distributed computing, and parallel programming.
  
The course is offered at undergraduate level.

## Notes on Online Courses

All students enrolled in this course have been added to the team in Microsoft Teams for the corresponding course number.
  
You can attend the online course in Microsoft Teams at the following channel:
[Team created for Big Data Analytics Course [Course Number: 308849]](https://teams.microsoft.com/l/team/19%3aPDANJpbakmpt4unmWVI4xuxdof5l4nDotl96Cf7cZ6w1%40thread.tacv2/conversations?groupId=dcb0d260-59d3-4610-b4e1-9d0d704041aa&tenantId=dfb5e216-2b8a-4b32-b1cb-e786a1095218)

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of our university, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Tue. and Fri. 9:10-12:00am
* Class Hours: Mon. 13:10-15:00pm, Wed. 15:10-16:00pm, **R427, 6th Teaching Building**
* TA: Mr. Chuang (R1424, Technology Building)
    
  E-mail: (TBA)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), database systems, discrete math (probability), operating systems,
  and working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: (Selected chapters)
  + Jiawei Han, Micheline Kamber and Jian Pei, Data Mining: Concepts and Techniques, 3rd ed., Morgan Kaufmann Publishers, July 2011. ([DM3])
* References:
  + Tom White, Hadoop: The Definitive Guide, 4th ed., O'Reilly Media, 2015.
  + Jimmy Lin and Chris Dyer, Data-Intensive Text Processing with MapReduce, Morgan & Claypool Publishers, 2010.
  + Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David Ullman, Mining of Massive Datasets, 3rd Edition, Cambridge University Press, Feb. 2020.
  + Jules S. Damji, Brooke Wenig, Tathagata Das, Denny Lee, Learning Spark: Lightning-Fast Big Data Analysis, 2nd ed., O'Reilly Media, Jul. 2020.
  + Others: online documents (Hadoop, Spark), tutorials, and selected academic papers.

# Informaiton Retrieval and Applications, Spring 2014

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in information
retrieval (IR), which is fundamental to modern Web search engines.
  
In addition to Web search, other applications of information retrieval
systems will also be described.
  
This year, the course is offered at graduate-level as well as the International Graduate Program in College of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS). It's taught in English.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang

# Introduction to Big Data Analytics, Spring 2019

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in big data analytics (BDA), which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major topics covered in this course will include: data mining, distributed computing, and parallel programming.
  
The course is offered at undergraduate level.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within our university, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Tue. and Fri. 9:10-12:00am
* Class Hours: Wed. 9:10-10:00am, Thu. 10:10-12:00am, **R1322, Technology Building**
* TA: Miss Chang (R1424, Technology Building)
    
  E-mail: (TBA)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), database systems, discrete math (probability), operating systems,
  and working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: (Selected chapters)
  + Jiawei Han, Micheline Kamber and Jian Pei, Data Mining: Concepts and Techniques, 3rd ed., Morgan Kaufmann Publishers, July 2011. ([DM3])
* References:
  + Tom White, Hadoop: The Definitive Guide, 4th ed., O'Reilly Media, 2015.
  + Jimmy Lin and Chris Dyer, Data-Intensive Text Processing with MapReduce, Morgan & Claypool Publishers, 2010.
  + Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David Ullman, Mining of Massive Datasets, 2nd Edition, Cambridge University Press, November 2014.
  + Holden Karau, Andy Konwinski, Patrick Wendell, Matei Zaharia, Learning Spark: Lightning-Fast Big Data Analysis, O'Reilly Media, January 2015.
  + Others: online documents (Hadoop, Spark), tutorials, and selected academic papers.

# Informaiton Retrieval and Applications, Fall 2020

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in information
retrieval (IR), which is fundamental to modern Web search engines.
  
In addition to Web search, other applications of information retrieval
systems will also be described.
  
This year, the course is offered at graduate-level as well as the International Graduate Program in College of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS). It's taught in English.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Mon. and Fri. 9:10-12:00am
* Class Hours: Tue. 15:10-18:00pm, *changed* to **R527, Sixth Teaching Building**
* TA: Mr. Wu (R1424, Technology Building)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), linear algebra
  and probability theory, working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan and Hinrich Schuetze, [Introduction to Information Retrieval](http://www.informationretrieval.org/), Cambridge University Press. 2008.
* References:
  + Bruce Croft, Donald Metzler, and Trevor Strohman, [Search Engines: Information Retrieval in Practice](http://www.search-engines-book.com/), Addison-Wesley, 2010
  + Ricardo Baeza-Yates and Berthier Ribeiro-Neto, [Modern Information Retrieval: The Concepts and Technology behind Search](http://www.mir2ed.org/),
    Addison-Wesley, 2011.
    - This is the second edition of their book [Modern Information Retrieval](http://people.ischool.berkeley.edu/~hearst/irbook/) in 1999.
  + Stefan Buettcher, Charles L.A. Clarke, and Gordon V. Cormack,
    [Information Retrieval: Implementing and Evaluating Search
    Engines](http://www.ir.uwaterloo.ca/book/), MIT Press, 2010.

# Operating Systems, Spring 2020 (Undergraduates)

This course offers an introduction to the concepts and principles in modern operating systems.
This is offered as a required course in the undergraduate program.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: jhwang AT csie DOT <the domain for our school>
* Office: R1534, Technology Building, ext. 4238
* Office Hours: Mon. 15:10-17:00pm, Tue. & Thu. 10:10-12:00am
* Class Hours: Wed. 10:10-12:00am, Thu. 9:10-10:00am, R327, 6th Teaching Building
* TA: (TBD) (R1424, Technology Building)
* Textbook: [Operating System Concepts, 9th ed.](http://os-book.com),
  by Abraham Silberschatz, Peter Baer Galvin, and Greg Gagne, John Wiley & Sons,
  Inc, 2013.
  + Note: The latest edition is the 10th edition, which is available as "Asia Edition" in Taiwan. I will try to cover some of the latest topics whenever possible.
  + Note: Earlier editions (such as 8th ed. or Java version) of the textbook
    is also acceptable. Changes in the latest edition will be mentioned
    whenever applicable.
  + Note: Please do not confuse it with the authors' new version called "Operating System Concepts Essentials, 2nd Edition", which covers most of the core topics in the contemporary
    operating systems. Some chapters were removed, such as Deadlocks, Virtual Machines, and Distributed Systems, some of which will still be covered in this course.
* References:
  + Modern Operating Systems, 4th ed., by Andrew S. Tanenbaum and Herbert Bos,
    Prentice-Hall, 2015.
    (MOS4)

# Informaiton Retrieval and Applications, Spring 2017

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in information
retrieval (IR), which is fundamental to modern Web search engines.
  
In addition to Web search, other applications of information retrieval
systems will also be described.
  
This year, the course is offered at graduate-level as well as the International Graduate Program in College of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS). It's taught in English.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Wed. and Thu. 2:00-5:00pm
* Class Hours: Fri. 9:10-12:00am, **R627, 6th Teaching Building**
* TA: Mr. Liu (s5548765 @ gmail . com) (R1424, Technology Building)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), linear algebra
  and probability theory, working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan and Hinrich Schuetze, [Introduction to Information Retrieval](http://www.informationretrieval.org/), Cambridge University Press. 2008.
* References:
  + Bruce Croft, Donald Metzler, and Trevor Strohman, [Search Engines: Information Retrieval in Practice](http://www.search-engines-book.com/), Addison-Wesley, 2010
  + Ricardo Baeza-Yates and Berthier Ribeiro-Neto, [Modern Information Retrieval: The Concepts and Technology behind Search](http://www.mir2ed.org/),
    Addison-Wesley, 2011.
    - This is the second edition of their book [Modern Information Retrieval](http://people.ischool.berkeley.edu/~hearst/irbook/) in 1999.
  + Stefan Buettcher, Charles L.A. Clarke, and Gordon V. Cormack,
    [Information Retrieval: Implementing and Evaluating Search
    Engines](http://www.ir.uwaterloo.ca/book/), MIT Press, 2010.

### Submission Instructions

**NOTE**: Programs or projects in electronic files must be submitted directly to the TA online.

If you cannot successfully submit your work, please contact with the TA or the instructor.

### Homeworks

# Informaiton Retrieval and Applications, Spring 2009

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in information retrieval.
In this course, you will learn the operations of modern Web search engines,
among other applications of information retrieval systems.

# Educational Data Mining and Applications, Fall 2024

This course is one of the advanced courses in the Micro Courses on Educational Big Data. It introduces the basic concepts and principles of data mining, including: data preprocessing, frequent pattern mining, classification, and clustering. Also, students need to use tools such as Weka and Python for educational big data analytics. With the programming exercises and term project, students will be able to learn the fundamental ideas of educational data analysis and real applications with practical tools.
  
The course is offered at undergraduate level.

## Notes on online recording of course sessions

All students enrolled in this course have been added to the team in Microsoft Teams for the corresponding course number.
  
You can check the online recording of the course in Microsoft Teams at the following channel:

* Class Hours: Mon. 10:10-12:00pm, Wed. 9:10-10:00am, **R234, Technology Building / R327, 6th Teaching Building**

The course is offered for the International Master Program in Financial Technology and Innovation Enterpreneur (IMFI).

# Informaiton Retrieval and Applications, Spring 2016

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in information
retrieval (IR), which is fundamental to modern Web search engines.
  
In addition to Web search, other applications of information retrieval
systems will also be described.
  
This year, the course is offered at graduate-level as well as the International Graduate Program in College of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS). It's taught in English.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Tue. and Thu. 9:00-12:00am
* Class Hours: Mon. 1:10-4:00pm, **R227, 6th Teaching Building**
* TA: Mr. Liu (s5548765 @ gmail . com) (R1424, Technology Building)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), linear algebra
  and probability theory, working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan and Hinrich Schuetze, [Introduction to Information Retrieval](http://www.informationretrieval.org/), Cambridge University Press. 2008.
* References:
  + Bruce Croft, Donald Metzler, and Trevor Strohman, [Search Engines: Information Retrieval in Practice](http://www.search-engines-book.com/), Addison-Wesley, 2010
  + Ricardo Baeza-Yates and Berthier Ribeiro-Neto, [Modern Information Retrieval: The Concepts and Technology behind Search](http://www.mir2ed.org/),
    Addison-Wesley, 2011.
    - This is the second edition of their book [Modern Information Retrieval](http://people.ischool.berkeley.edu/~hearst/irbook/) in 1999.
  + Stefan Buettcher, Charles L.A. Clarke, and Gordon V. Cormack,
    [Information Retrieval: Implementing and Evaluating Search
    Engines](http://www.ir.uwaterloo.ca/book/), MIT Press, 2010.

## Useful Links

Here're some useful links to information retrieval related resources or further readings.

* Generic
  + [Wikipedia page on Information Retrieval](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Information_retrieval)
  + [Information Retrieval Resources](http://www-csli.stanford.edu/~hinrich/information-retrieval.html)
* Some open source tools
  + Apache Lucene/Solr
  + Apache Hadoop, Spark
  + Lemur Project, Indri, Galago
  + Terrier
* Academic
  + International Conferences:
    - ACM SIGIR: International Conference on Information Retrieval
    - ACM CIKM: Conference on Information Knowledge and Management
    - WWW: International World Wide Web Conference
    - JCDL: Joint Conference on Digital Libraries
    - TREC: TExt Retrieval Conference
  + Regional Conferences:
    - AIRS: Asia Information Retrieval Symposium
    - ECIR: European Conference on Information Retrieval
    - ECDL: European Conference on Research and Advanced Technology for Digital Libraries
    - ICADL: International Conference on Asian Digital Libraries
  + Journals:
    - ACM TOIS: Transactions on Information Systems
    - JASIST: Journal of the American Society of Information Sciences
    - IP&M: Information Processing and Management
  + Others:
    - WI: International Conference on Web intelligence
    - KDD: ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining
    - ICDM: International Conference on Data Mining
    - IEEE TKDE: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
* More Books
  + Managing Gigabytes. I.H. Witten, A. Moffat, T.C. Bell. Morgan Kaufmann, 1999.
  + Information Retrieval. C. J. van Rijsbergen. Butterworths, 1979.
  + Readings in Information Retrieval. K. Sparck Jones, P. Willett. Morgan Kaufmann, 1997.
  + Automatic Text Processing, G. Salton, Addison-Wesley, 1989.

# Educational Data Mining and Applications, Fall 2023

This course is one of the advanced courses in the Micro Courses on Educational Big Data. It introduces the basic concepts and principles of data mining, including: data preprocessing, frequent pattern mining, classification, and clustering. Also, students need to use tools such as Weka and Python for educational big data analytics. With the programming exercises and term project, students will be able to learn the fundamental ideas of educational data analysis and real applications with practical tools.
  
The course is offered at undergraduate level.

# Big Data Mining and Applications, Fall 2024

This course discusses issues in scalable mining of big data in various applications, which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major focus of this course will be utilizing parallel progamming in distributed platforms for efficient mining of different kinds of big data.
  
The course is offered at graduate-level, and will be taught in English.

## Notes on Online Sessions of this Course

All students enrolled in this course have been added to the two separate teams in Microsoft Teams for the corresponding course numbers: 333586, 336185.
  
The major link to the online course sessions and recordings in Microsft Teams at:
[the team created for Big Data Mining [course number: 336185]](https://teams.microsoft.com/l/team/19%3A8I-6eE-HNGH9etrDtd8jUrlzDfiNkv_hM4VP31RsNOA1%40thread.tacv2/conversations?groupId=6fc9016a-4347-4d1f-8581-271fdd7b9eef&tenantId=dfb5e216-2b8a-4b32-b1cb-e786a1095218)

**[NOTE]** For those who really cannot attend the class in person due to some unforeseeable reasons, you could join the corresponding online sessions in Teams.
During course sessions, remember to sign up your name and student ID in Teams, when the TA will remind you.

Please read the notes to the course very carefully about the requirements for the programming homework and quiz.
[\*\*\* NOTE \*\*\*]: This course is *NOT* an introductory course for undergraduates.
You are strongly suggested to take the first course of "Introduction to Big Data Analytcis" or "Data Mining" in the undergraduate-level as a prerequisite.
  
**It contains very heavy loads of parallel programming, which is a totally different concept from sequential programming**.
  
Please think twice before taking this course!

## Programming Assignments and Projects

Please hand in your assignment before deadline according to the following instructions.   

### Program Submission Instructions

**NOTE**: Programs or projects in electronic files must be submitted directly to the TA online at
iSchool+.

If you cannot successfully submit your work, please contact with the TA or the instructor.

### Distributed Programming Environment

You can use your own computers, or virtual machines on any cloud computing services to setup your distributed programming environment for this course.

1. **Computers**:
     
   You can build your own distributed environment by commodity computers.
   Usually, Hadoop/Spark does not require very poweful capability of each machine.
2. **VMs on the cloud**:
     
   You can use existing cloud computing services from Google, Amazon, or Microsoft Azure to setup distributed environemnt.
   Most cloud services provide free quota for limited usage (CPU, memory, network bandwidth, storage).

### Homework

There will be about five programming exercises (and some written exercises) that target at different data analysis tasks.
  
**Given large datasets, all homeworks require parallel programming using either Hadoop or Spark, in *distributed mode*.** (for CS students)
  
Since it's totallly different from sequential programming, it takes much more efforts and time to complete.
Please think twice before taking this course!

In the case of very large datasets, you still need to analyze all data objects in the whole dataset.
Please design your program accordingly to first partition the data into several batches, and then merge the final result.

1. HW#0
   :
   Big data analysis platform setup & simple statistics in MapReduce (or Python in Jupyter Notebook)
     
   Due: *Oct. 16, 2024*
2. HW#1

   Due: *Oct. 30, 2024*

   Due: *Nov. 20, 2024*
4. HW#3

   Due: extended to *Dec. 11, 2024*
5. HW#4

   Due: *Dec. 25, 2024*
6. HW#5 [Optional]

# Educational Data Mining and Applications, Fall 2025

This course is one of the advanced courses in the Micro Courses on Educational Big Data. It introduces the basic concepts and principles of data mining, including: data preprocessing, frequent pattern mining, classification, and clustering. Also, students need to use tools such as Weka and Python for educational big data analytics. With the programming exercises and term project, students will be able to learn the fundamental ideas of educational data analysis and real applications with practical tools.
  
The course is offered at undergraduate level.

# Big Data Mining and Applications, Fall 2019

This course discusses issues in scalable mining of big data in various applications, which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major focus in this course will be utilizing parallel progamming in distributed platforms for efficient mining of different kinds of big data.
  
The course is offered at graduate-level, and will be taught in English.

[\*\*\* NOTE \*\*\*]: This course is *NOT* an introductory course for undergraduates.
You are required to take the first course of "Introduction to Big Data Analytcis" or "Data Mining" in the undergraduate-level as as prerequisite.
  
**It contains very heavy loads of parallel programming, which is a totally different concept from sequential programming**.
  
Please think twice before taking this course!

Please read the [notes to the course](https://140.124.180.184:8081/Course/BDM_Fall19/slides/Notes.pptx) very carefully about the requirements for the programming homework and quiz.

* Class Hours: Fri. 9:10-12:00am, **R325, 6th Teaching Building**
* TA: Mr. Chou (R1424, Technology Building)
    
   e-mail: (TBA)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), database systems, discrete math (probability), linear algebra, basic data mining,
  and working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: (Selected chapters)
  + Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David Ullman, Mining of Massive Datasets, 2nd Edition, Cambridge University Press, November 2014. ([MMDS2])
  + Jimmy Lin and Chris Dyer, Data-Intensive Text Processing with MapReduce, Morgan & Claypool Publishers, 2010. ([MR])
* References:
  + Jiawei Han, Micheline Kamber and Jian Pei, Data Mining: Concepts and Techniques, 3rd ed., Morgan Kaufmann Publishers, July 2011. ([DM3])

  + Others: Web documents, tutorials, and selected academic papers.

# Big Data Mining and Applications, Spring 2018

This course discusses issues in scalable mining of big data in various applications, which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major focus in this course will be utilizing parallel progamming in distributed platforms for efficient mining of different kinds of big data.
  
The course is offered at graduate-level, and will be taught in English.

[\*\*\* NOTE \*\*\*]: This course is *NOT* an introductory course for undergraduates.
You are required to take the first course of "Introduction to Big Data Analytcis" or "Data Mining" in the undergraduate-level as as prerequisite.
  
**It contains very heavy loads of parallel programming, which is a totally different concept from sequential programming**.
  
Please think twice before taking this course!

Please read the [notes to the course](http://140.124.180.184/Course/BDM_Spring18/slides/Notes.pptx) very carefully about the requirements for the programming homework and quiz.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Mon. & Wed. 4:10-5:00pm
* Class Hours: Fri. 9:10-12:00am, **R327, 6th Teaching Building**
* TA: Jack Lin (R1424, Technology Building)
    
   e-mail: jack888835 AT GMail DOT COM
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), database systems, discrete math (probability), linear algebra, basic data mining,
  and working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: (Selected chapters)
  + Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David Ullman, Mining of Massive Datasets, 2nd Edition, Cambridge University Press, November 2014. ([MMDS2])
  + Jimmy Lin and Chris Dyer, Data-Intensive Text Processing with MapReduce, Morgan & Claypool Publishers, 2010. ([MR])
* References:
  + Jiawei Han, Micheline Kamber and Jian Pei, Data Mining: Concepts and Techniques, 3rd ed., Morgan Kaufmann Publishers, July 2011. ([DM3])
  + Tom White, Hadoop: The Definitive Guide, 4th ed., O'Reilly Media, 2015.
  + Holden Karau, Andy Konwinski, Patrick Wendell, Matei Zaharia, Learning Spark: Lightning-Fast Big Data Analysis, O'Reilly Media, January 2015.
  + Others: Web documents, tutorials, and selected academic papers.

# Operating Systems, Spring 2026 (Undergraduates)

This course introduces the basic concepts and principles in modern operating systems.
This is offered as a required course in the undergraduate program.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: jhwang AT <the domain for our school>
* Office: R1534, Technology Building, ext. 4238
* Office Hours: Tue & Fri. 9:10-12:00am
* Class Hours: Tue. 13:10-15:00pm, Thu. 9:10-10:00am, **R327, Sixth Teaching Building** (originally R501, Pioneer Building)
* TA: Mr. Chiu & Mr. Ke (R1424, Technology Building)
* Textbook: [Operating System Concepts, 10th ed.](https://os-book.com),
  by Abraham Silberschatz, Peter Baer Galvin, and Greg Gagne, John Wiley & Sons,
  Inc, 2019.
  + Note: Earlier editions (such as 9th ed. or Java version) of the textbook
    is also acceptable. Changes in the latest edition will be mentioned
    whenever applicable.
  + Note: Please do not confuse it with the authors' new version called "Operating System Concepts Essentials, 2nd Edition", which covers most of the core topics in the contemporary
    operating systems. Some chapters were removed, such as Deadlocks, Virtual Machines, and Distributed Systems, some of which will still be covered in this course.
* References:
  + Modern Operating Systems, 5th ed., by Andrew S. Tanenbaum and Herbert Bos,
    Prentice-Hall, 2022.
    (MOS5)
  + Ann McHoes, Ida M. Flynn, Understanding Operating Systems, 8th Ed., Cengage Learning, 2017.

# Informaiton Retrieval and Applications, Spring 2011

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in information
retrieval, which is fundamental to modern Web search engines.
In addition to Web search, other applications of information retrieval
systems will also be described.
This course will be taught in English.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Wed. and Thu. 10:00-12:00am
* Class Hours: Wed. 13:10-16:00pm, R327, 6th Teaching Building
* TA: Mr. C.H.Lin (R1424, Technology Building)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), linear algebra
  and probability theory, working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan and Hinrich Schuetze, [Introduction to Information Retrieval](http://www.informationretrieval.org/), Cambridge University Press. 2008.
* References:
  + Ricardo Baeza-Yates and Berthier Ribeiro-Neto, [Modern Information Retrieval: The Concepts and Technology behind Search](http://www.mir2ed.org/),
    Addison-Wesley, 2011.
    - This is the second edition of their book [Modern Information Retrieval](http://people.ischool.berkeley.edu/~hearst/irbook/) in 1999.
  + Stefan Buettcher, Charles L.A. Clarke, and Gordon V. Cormack,
    [Information Retrieval: Implementing and Evaluating Search
    Engines](http://www.ir.uwaterloo.ca/book/), MIT Press, 2010.
  + Bruce Croft, Donald Metzler, and Trevor Strohman, [Search Engines: Information Retrieval in Practice](http://www.search-engines-book.com/), Addison-Wesley, 2010

# Operating Systems, Spring 2023 (Undergraduates)

This course offers an introduction to the concepts and principles in modern operating systems.
This is offered as a required course in the undergraduate program.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: jhwang AT <the domain for our school>
* Office: R1534, Technology Building, ext. 4238
* Office Hours: Tue & Fri. 9:10-12:00am
* Class Hours: Mon. 13:10-15:00pm, Thu. 9:10-10:00am, R327, 6th Teaching Building
* TA: Mr. Chuang (R1424, Technology Building)
* Textbook: [Operating System Concepts, 9th ed.](https://os-book.com),
  by Abraham Silberschatz, Peter Baer Galvin, and Greg Gagne, John Wiley & Sons,
  Inc, 2013.
  + Note: The latest edition is the 10th edition, which is available as "Asia Edition" in Taiwan. I will try to cover some of the latest topics whenever possible.
  + Note: Earlier editions (such as 8th ed. or Java version) of the textbook
    is also acceptable. Changes in the latest edition will be mentioned
    whenever applicable.
  + Note: Please do not confuse it with the authors' new version called "Operating System Concepts Essentials, 2nd Edition", which covers most of the core topics in the contemporary
    operating systems. Some chapters were removed, such as Deadlocks, Virtual Machines, and Distributed Systems, some of which will still be covered in this course.
* References:
  + Modern Operating Systems, 4th ed., by Andrew S. Tanenbaum and Herbert Bos,
    Prentice-Hall, 2015.
    (MOS4)

# Informaiton Retrieval and Applications, Spring 2019

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in information
retrieval (IR), which is fundamental to modern Web search engines.
  
In addition to Web search, other applications of information retrieval
systems will also be described.
  
This year, the course is offered at graduate-level as well as the International Graduate Program in College of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS). It's taught in English.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Tue. and Fri. 9:10-12:00am
* Class Hours: Mon. 15:10-18:00pm, **R327, 6th Teaching Building**
* TA: Mr. Wu (R1424, Technology Building)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), linear algebra
  and probability theory, working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan and Hinrich Schuetze, [Introduction to Information Retrieval](http://www.informationretrieval.org/), Cambridge University Press. 2008.
* References:
  + Bruce Croft, Donald Metzler, and Trevor Strohman, [Search Engines: Information Retrieval in Practice](http://www.search-engines-book.com/), Addison-Wesley, 2010
  + Ricardo Baeza-Yates and Berthier Ribeiro-Neto, [Modern Information Retrieval: The Concepts and Technology behind Search](http://www.mir2ed.org/),
    Addison-Wesley, 2011.
    - This is the second edition of their book [Modern Information Retrieval](http://people.ischool.berkeley.edu/~hearst/irbook/) in 1999.
  + Stefan Buettcher, Charles L.A. Clarke, and Gordon V. Cormack,
    [Information Retrieval: Implementing and Evaluating Search
    Engines](http://www.ir.uwaterloo.ca/book/), MIT Press, 2010.

# Introduction to Big Data Analytics, Fall 2020

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in big data analytics (BDA), which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major topics covered in this course will include: data mining, distributed computing, and parallel programming.
  
The course is offered at undergraduate level.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within our university, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Mon. and Fri. 9:10-12:00am
* Class Hours: Mon. 13:10-15:00pm, Wed. 15:10-16:00pm, **R325, 6th Teaching Building**
* TA: Mr. Chen (R1424, Technology Building)
    
  E-mail: (TBA)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), database systems, discrete math (probability), operating systems,
  and working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: (Selected chapters)
  + Jiawei Han, Micheline Kamber and Jian Pei, Data Mining: Concepts and Techniques, 3rd ed., Morgan Kaufmann Publishers, July 2011. ([DM3])
* References:
  + Tom White, Hadoop: The Definitive Guide, 4th ed., O'Reilly Media, 2015.
  + Jimmy Lin and Chris Dyer, Data-Intensive Text Processing with MapReduce, Morgan & Claypool Publishers, 2010.
  + Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David Ullman, Mining of Massive Datasets, 2nd Edition, Cambridge University Press, November 2014.
  + Holden Karau, Andy Konwinski, Patrick Wendell, Matei Zaharia, Learning Spark: Lightning-Fast Big Data Analysis, O'Reilly Media, January 2015.
  + Others: online documents (Hadoop, Spark), tutorials, and selected academic papers.

# Big Data Mining and Applications, Fall 2025

This course discusses issues in scalable mining of big data in various applications, which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major focus of this course will be utilizing parallel progamming in distributed platforms for efficient mining of different kinds of big data.
  
The course is offered at graduate-level, and will be taught in English.

## Notes on Online Sessions of this Course

All students enrolled in this course will be added to the teams in Microsoft Teams for the corresponding course numbers: 347338, 350369.
  
The channel to use for the team in this course Big Data Mining [course number: 350369].

**[NOTE]** For those who really cannot attend the class in person due to some unforeseeable reasons, you could join the corresponding online sessions in Teams.
During course sessions, remember to sign up your name and student ID in Teams, when the TA will remind you.

Please read the notes to the course very carefully about the requirements for the programming homework and quiz.
[\*\*\* NOTE \*\*\*]: This course is *NOT* an introductory course for undergraduates.
You are strongly suggested to take the first course of "Introduction to Big Data Analytcis" or "Data Mining" in the undergraduate-level as a prerequisite.
  
**It contains very heavy loads of parallel programming, which is a totally different concept from sequential programming**.
  
Please think twice before taking this course!

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)

* Class Hours: Mon. 15:10pm-18:00pm, **R401, Pioneer Building** (effective from Sep. 15, 2025)
* TA: Mr. Lin (R1424, Technology Building)
    
   e-mail: (TBA)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), database systems, discrete math (probability), linear algebra, basic data mining,
  and working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: (Selected chapters)
  + Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David Ullman, [Mining of Massive Datasets](http://www.mmds.org/), 3rd Edition, Cambridge University Press, Feb. 2020. ([MMDS3])
  + Jimmy Lin and Chris Dyer, Data-Intensive Text Processing with MapReduce, Morgan & Claypool Publishers, 2010. ([MR])
* References:
  + Jiawei Han, Jian Pei, and Hanghang Tong, Data Mining: Concepts and Techniques, 4th ed., Morgan Kaufmann Publishers, Oct. 2022. ([DM4])
  + Tom White, Hadoop: The Definitive Guide, 4th ed., O'Reilly Media, 2015.
  + Holden Karau, Andy Konwinski, Patrick Wendell, Matei Zaharia, Learning Spark: Lightning-Fast Big Data Analysis, O'Reilly Media, January 2015.
  + Others: Web documents, tutorials, and selected academic papers.

## Programming Assignments and Projects

Please hand in your assignment before deadline according to the following instructions.   

### Program Submission Instructions

**NOTE**: Programs or projects in electronic files must be submitted directly to the TA online at
iSchool+.

If you cannot successfully submit your work, please contact with the TA or the instructor.

### Distributed Programming Environment

You can use your own computers, or virtual machines on any cloud computing services to setup your distributed programming environment for this course.

1. **Computers**:
     
   You can build your own distributed environment by commodity computers.
   Usually, Hadoop/Spark does not require very poweful capability of each machine.
2. **VMs on the cloud**:
     
   You can use existing cloud computing services from Google, Amazon, or Microsoft Azure to setup distributed environemnt.
   Most cloud services provide free quota for limited usage (CPU, memory, network bandwidth, storage).

### Homework

There will be about 5 programming exercises (and some written exercises) that target at different data analysis tasks.
  
**Given large datasets, all homeworks require parallel programming using either Hadoop or Spark, in *distributed mode*.** (for CS students)
  
Since it's totallly different from sequential programming, it takes much more efforts and time to complete.
Please think twice before taking this course!

In the case of very large datasets, you still need to analyze all data objects in the whole dataset.
Please design your program accordingly to first partition the data into several batches, and then merge the final result.

# Operating Systems, Fall 2020 (EECS Undergraduates)

This course offers an introduction to the concepts and principles in modern operating systems.
This is offered as a required course in the undergraduate program of EECS.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: jhwang AT csie DOT <the domain for our school>
* Office: R1534, Technology Building, ext. 4238
* Office Hours: Mon. 15:10-17:00pm, Mon. & Fri. 9:10-12:00am
* Class Hours: Wed. 9:10-12:00am, R226, 6th Teaching Building
* TA: Tim (R1424, Technology Building)
* Textbook: [Operating System Concepts, 9th ed.](http://os-book.com),
  by Abraham Silberschatz, Peter Baer Galvin, and Greg Gagne, John Wiley & Sons,
  Inc, 2013.
  + Note: The latest edition is the 10th edition, which is available as "Asia Edition" in Taiwan. I will try to cover some of the latest topics whenever possible.
  + Note: Earlier editions (such as 8th ed. or Java version) of the textbook
    is also acceptable. Changes in the latest edition will be mentioned
    whenever applicable.
  + Note: Please do not confuse it with the authors' new version called "Operating System Concepts Essentials, 2nd Edition", which covers most of the core topics in the contemporary
    operating systems. Some chapters were removed, such as Deadlocks, Virtual Machines, and Distributed Systems, some of which will still be covered in this course.
* References:
  + Modern Operating Systems, 4th ed., by Andrew S. Tanenbaum and Herbert Bos,
    Prentice-Hall, 2015.
    (MOS4)

# Informaiton Retrieval and Applications, Spring 2012

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in information
retrieval, which is fundamental to modern Web search engines.
In addition to Web search, other applications of information retrieval
systems will also be described.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Tue. and Wed. 9:00-12:00am
* Class Hours: Thu. 9:10-12:00am, R1322, Technology Building
* TA: Mr. H.C.Liu (R1424, Technology Building)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), linear algebra
  and probability theory, working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan and Hinrich Schuetze, [Introduction to Information Retrieval](http://www.informationretrieval.org/), Cambridge University Press. 2008.
* References:
  + Ricardo Baeza-Yates and Berthier Ribeiro-Neto, [Modern Information Retrieval: The Concepts and Technology behind Search](http://www.mir2ed.org/),
    Addison-Wesley, 2011.
    - This is the second edition of their book [Modern Information Retrieval](http://people.ischool.berkeley.edu/~hearst/irbook/) in 1999.
  + Stefan Buettcher, Charles L.A. Clarke, and Gordon V. Cormack,
    [Information Retrieval: Implementing and Evaluating Search
    Engines](http://www.ir.uwaterloo.ca/book/), MIT Press, 2010.
  + Bruce Croft, Donald Metzler, and Trevor Strohman, [Search Engines: Information Retrieval in Practice](http://www.search-engines-book.com/), Addison-Wesley, 2010

## Useful Links

Here're some useful links to information retrieval related resources or further readings.

* Generic
  + [Wikipedia page on Information Retrieval](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Information_retrieval)
  + [Information Retrieval Resources](http://www-csli.stanford.edu/~hinrich/information-retrieval.html)
* Academic
  + International Conferences:
    - ACM SIGIR: International Conference on Information Retrieval
    - ACM CIKM: Conference on Information Knowledge and Management
    - WWW: International World Wide Web Conference
    - JCDL: Joint Conference on Digital Libraries
    - TREC: TExt Retrieval Conference
  + Regional Conferences:
    - AIRS: Asia Information Retrieval Symposium
    - ECIR: European Conference on Information Retrieval
    - ECDL: European Conference on Research and Advanced Technology for Digital Libraries
    - ICADL: International Conference on Asian Digital Libraries
  + Journals:
    - ACM TOIS: Transactions on Information Systems
    - JASIST: Journal of the American Society of Information Sciences
    - IP&M: Information Processing and Management
  + Others:
    - WI: International Conference on Web intelligence
    - KDD: ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining
    - ICDM: International Conference on Data Mining
    - IEEE TKDE: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
* More Books
  + Managing Gigabytes. I.H. Witten, A. Moffat, T.C. Bell. Morgan Kaufmann, 1999.
  + Information Retrieval. C. J. van Rijsbergen. Butterworths, 1979.
  + Readings in Information Retrieval. K. Sparck Jones, P. Willett. Morgan Kaufmann, 1997.
  + Automatic Text Processing, G. Salton, Addison-Wesley, 1989.

# Introduction to Big Data Analytics, Spring 2017

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in big data analytics (BDA), which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major topics covered in this course will include: data mining, distributed computing, and parallel programming.
  
The course is offered at junior-level.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Wed. and Thu. 2:00-5:00pm
* Class Hours: Tue. 2:10-4:00pm, Fri. 3:10-4:00pm, **R227, 6th Teaching Building**
* TA: Mr. Lin (R1424, Technology Building)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), database systems,
  and working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: (Selected chapters)
  + Jiawei Han, Micheline Kamber and Jian Pei, Data Mining: Concepts and Techniques, 3rd ed., Morgan Kaufmann Publishers, July 2011. ([DM3])
  + Tom White, Hadoop: The Definitive Guide, 4th ed., O'Reilly Media, 2015.
* References:
  + Jimmy Lin and Chris Dyer, Data-Intensive Text Processing with MapReduce, Morgan & Claypool Publishers, 2010.
  + Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David Ullman, Mining of Massive Datasets, 2nd Edition, Cambridge University Press, November 2014.
  + Holden Karau, Andy Konwinski, Patrick Wendell, Matei Zaharia, Learning Spark: Lightning-Fast Big Data Analysis, O'Reilly Media, January 2015.
  + Others: Web documents, tutorials, and selected academic papers.

# Introduction to Big Data Analytics, Spring 2018

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in big data analytics (BDA), which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major topics covered in this course will include: data mining, distributed computing, and parallel programming.
  
The course is offered at undergraduate-level.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Mon. and Wed. 4:10-6:00pm
* Class Hours: Tue. 9:10-10:00am, Wed. 10:10-12:00am, **R227/R327, 6th Teaching Building**
* TA: Miss Lu (R1424, Technology Building)
    
  E-mail: t106598005 AT NTUT . ORG DOT TW

  and working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: (Selected chapters)

  + Tom White, Hadoop: The Definitive Guide, 4th ed., O'Reilly Media, 2015.
* References:
  + Jimmy Lin and Chris Dyer, Data-Intensive Text Processing with MapReduce, Morgan & Claypool Publishers, 2010.
  + Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David Ullman, Mining of Massive Datasets, 2nd Edition, Cambridge University Press, November 2014.
  + Holden Karau, Andy Konwinski, Patrick Wendell, Matei Zaharia, Learning Spark: Lightning-Fast Big Data Analysis, O'Reilly Media, January 2015.
  + Others: online documents (Hadoop, Spark), tutorials, and selected academic papers.

# Big Data Mining and Applications, Fall 2023

This course discusses issues in scalable mining of big data in various applications, which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major focus of this course will be utilizing parallel progamming in distributed platforms for efficient mining of different kinds of big data.
  
The course is offered at graduate-level, and will be taught in English.

## Notes on Online Sessions of this Course

All students enrolled in this course have been added to the two separate teams in Microsoft Teams for the corresponding course numbers: 321524, 323392.
  
The major link to the online course sessions and recordings in Microsft Teams at:
[the team created for Big Data Mining [course number: 321524]](https://teams.microsoft.com/l/team/19%3aPPLNPevIsFh3N94apM0pJhTnQnqfuUr6VXKQCrbFJBg1%40thread.tacv2/conversations?groupId=c8540540-f7d0-4789-99a7-08217b1b3ec7&tenantId=dfb5e216-2b8a-4b32-b1cb-e786a1095218)

**[NOTE]** For those who really cannot attend the class in person due to some unforeseeable reasons, you could join the corresponding online sessions in Teams.
During course sessions, remember to sign up your name and student ID in Teams, when the TA will remind you.

Please read the notes to the course very carefully about the requirements for the programming homework and quiz.
[\*\*\* NOTE \*\*\*]: This course is *NOT* an introductory course for undergraduates.
You are strongly suggested to take the first course of "Introduction to Big Data Analytcis" or "Data Mining" in the undergraduate-level as a prerequisite.
  
**It contains very heavy loads of parallel programming, which is a totally different concept from sequential programming**.
  
Please think twice before taking this course!

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)

* Class Hours: Thu. 9:10am-12:00pm, **R231, Technology Building**
* TA: Lawrence (R1424, Technology Building)
    
   e-mail: (TBA)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), database systems, discrete math (probability), linear algebra, basic data mining,
  and working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: (Selected chapters)
  + Jure Leskovec, Anand Rajaraman, Jeffrey David Ullman, [Mining of Massive Datasets](http://www.mmds.org/), 3rd Edition, Cambridge University Press, Feb. 2020. ([MMDS3])
  + Jimmy Lin and Chris Dyer, Data-Intensive Text Processing with MapReduce, Morgan & Claypool Publishers, 2010. ([MR])

  + Jiawei Han, Jian Pei, and Hanghang Tong, Data Mining: Concepts and Techniques, 4th ed., Morgan Kaufmann Publishers, Oct. 2022. ([DM4])

  + Others: Web documents, tutorials, and selected academic papers.

## Programming Assignments and Projects

Please hand in your assignment before deadline according to the following instructions.   

### Program Submission Instructions

**NOTE**: Programs or projects in electronic files must be submitted directly to the TA online at
iSchool+.

If you cannot successfully submit your work, please contact with the TA or the instructor.

### Distributed Programming Environment

You can use your own computers, or virtual machines on any cloud computing services to setup your distributed programming environment for this course.

1. **Computers**:
     
   You can build your own distributed environment by commodity computers.
   Usually, Hadoop/Spark does not require very poweful capability of each machine.
2. **VMs on the cloud**:
     
   You can use existing cloud computing services from Google, Amazon, or Microsoft Azure to setup distributed environemnt.
   Most cloud services provide free quota for limited usage (CPU, memory, network bandwidth, storage).

### Homework

There will be about five programming exercises (and some written exercises) that target at different data analysis tasks.
  
**Given large datasets, all homeworks require parallel programming using either Hadoop or Spark, in *distributed mode*.** (for CS students)
  
Since it's totallly different from sequential programming, it takes much more efforts and time to complete.
Please think twice before taking this course!

In the case of very large datasets, you still need to analyze all data objects in the whole dataset.
Please design your program accordingly to first partition the data into several batches, and then merge the final result.

1. [HW#0](HW0.pptx)
   :
   Big data analysis platform setup & simple statistics in MapReduce (or Python in Jupyter Notebook)
     
   Due: **Oct. 19, 2023**
2. [HW#1](HW1.pptx)
   :
   Analyzing documents in MapReduce (or Python in Jupyter Notebook)
     
   Due: **Nov. 2, 2023**
3. [HW#2](HW2.pptx)
   :
   Statistics (counting, co-occurrence) of various data types in MapReduce (or Python in Jupyter Notebook)
     
   Due: **Nov. 23, 2023**
4. [HW#3](HW3.pptx)
   :
   Finding similar documents (by calculating minhash signatures, LSH, and KNN search) in MapReduce (or Python in Jupyter Notebook)
     
   Due: **Dec. 7, 2023**
5. [HW#4](HW4.pptx)
   :
   Recommender systems using collaborative filtering in MapReduce (or Python in Jupyter Notebook)
     
   Due: **Jan. 4, 2024**
6. [HW#5 (Optional)](HW5.pptx)
   :
   Analyzing web graphs in MapReduce (Connectivity, PageRank, ...)
     
   Due: **Jan. 4, 2024**

### Projects

*Notes*: About the datasets available for potential topics in term project, please check information about [recent competitions](competition.pptx).

The final report should contain at least the following: 1. *presentation slides* (for all teams), and 2. *source code, installation/execution instructions,    team members and task responsibility* (for system projects) | **Jan. 12, 2024** (Fri.) |

# Big Data Mining and Applications, Fall 2021

This course discusses issues in scalable mining of big data in various applications, which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major focus in this course will be utilizing parallel progamming in distributed platforms for efficient mining of different kinds of big data.
  
The course is offered at graduate-level, and will be taught in English.

## Notes on Online Courses

All students enrolled in this course have been added to the team in Microsoft Teams.
  
You can attend the online course in Microsft Teams at:
[the team created for Big Data Mining [course number: 293679]](https://teams.microsoft.com/l/team/19%3aSvPZqhlsCr2YI31aSEF68HwI6VKVfh3kL5UzESSYG-Y1%40thread.tacv2/conversations?groupId=7f734b07-df5a-4d35-8146-7ddc47d804c7&tenantId=dfb5e216-2b8a-4b32-b1cb-e786a1095218)

[\*\*\* NOTE \*\*\*]: This course is *NOT* an introductory course for undergraduates.
You are required to take the first course of "Introduction to Big Data Analytcis" or "Data Mining" in the undergraduate-level as a prerequisite.
  
**It contains very heavy loads of parallel programming, which is a totally different concept from sequential programming**.
  
Please think twice before taking this course!

Please read the [notes to the course](https://140.124.180.184:8081/Course/BDM_Fall21/slides/Notes.pptx) very carefully about the requirements for the programming homework and quiz.

## Course Information

## Programming Assignments and Projects

### Program Submission Instructions

**NOTE**: Programs or projects in electronic files must be submitted directly to the TA online at
iSchool+.

If you cannot successfully submit your work, please contact with the TA or the instructor.

### Distributed Programming Environment

You can use your own computers, or virtual machines on any cloud computing services to setup your distributed programming environment for this course.

1. **Computers**:
     
   You can build your own distributed environment by commodity computers.
   Usually, Hadoop/Spark does not require very poweful capability of each machine.
2. **VMs on the cloud**:
     
   You can use existing cloud computing services from Google, Amazon, or Microsoft Azure to setup distributed environemnt.
   Most cloud services provide free quota for limited usage (CPU, memory, network bandwidth, storage).

### Homework

There will be about five programming exercises (and some written exercises) that target at different data analysis tasks.
  
**Given large dataset, all homeworkas require parallel programming using either Hadoop or Spark, in *distributed mode*.** (for CS students)
  
Since it's totallly different from sequential programming, it takes much more efforts and time to complete.
Please think twice before taking this course!

In the case of very large datasets, you still need to analyze all data objects in the whole dataset.
Please design your program accordingly to partition the data into several batches, and merge the final result.

# Informaiton Retrieval and Applications, Spring 2015

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in information
retrieval (IR), which is fundamental to modern Web search engines.
  
In addition to Web search, other applications of information retrieval
systems will also be described.
  
This year, the course is offered at graduate-level as well as the International Graduate Program in College of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS). It's taught in English.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Tue. and Thu. 9:00-12:00am
* Class Hours: Mon. 9:10-12:00am, **R627, 6th Teaching Building**
* TA: Mr. Tsai (R1424, Technology Building)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), linear algebra
  and probability theory, working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan and Hinrich Schuetze, [Introduction to Information Retrieval](http://www.informationretrieval.org/), Cambridge University Press. 2008.
* References:
  + Bruce Croft, Donald Metzler, and Trevor Strohman, [Search Engines: Information Retrieval in Practice](http://www.search-engines-book.com/), Addison-Wesley, 2010
  + Ricardo Baeza-Yates and Berthier Ribeiro-Neto, [Modern Information Retrieval: The Concepts and Technology behind Search](http://www.mir2ed.org/),
    Addison-Wesley, 2011.
    - This is the second edition of their book [Modern Information Retrieval](http://people.ischool.berkeley.edu/~hearst/irbook/) in 1999.
  + Stefan Buettcher, Charles L.A. Clarke, and Gordon V. Cormack,
    [Information Retrieval: Implementing and Evaluating Search
    Engines](http://www.ir.uwaterloo.ca/book/), MIT Press, 2010.

# Informaiton Retrieval and Applications, Spring 2013

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in information
retrieval (IR), which is fundamental to modern Web search engines.
  
In addition to Web search, other applications of information retrieval
systems will also be described.
  
This year, the course is offered at graduate-level as well as the International Graduate Program in College of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science (EECS). It's taught in English.

## Course Information

* Instructor: Jenq-Haur Wang
* E-mail: mailbox at the domain of csie within NTUT, and my username is jhwang
* Office: R1534, Technology Building (ext. 4238)
* Office Hours: Tue. and Wed. 9:00-12:00am
* Class Hours: Wed. 1:10-4:00pm, R325, Sixth Academic Building
* TA: Ting-Wei (at R1424, Technology Building)
* Prerequisite: data structures (and algorithms), linear algebra
  and probability theory, working experience of high-level programming languages
* Textbook: Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan and Hinrich Schuetze, [Introduction to Information Retrieval](http://www.informationretrieval.org/), Cambridge University Press. 2008.
* References:
  + Ricardo Baeza-Yates and Berthier Ribeiro-Neto, [Modern Information Retrieval: The Concepts and Technology behind Search](http://www.mir2ed.org/),
    Addison-Wesley, 2011.

retrieval (IR), which is fundamental to modern Web search engines.

systems will also be described.

# Introduction to Big Data Analytics, Fall 2021

This course offers an introduction to the principles and concepts in big data analytics (BDA), which is gaining more popularity in recent years.
  
The major topics covered in this course will include: data mining, distributed computing, and parallel programming.
  
The course is offered at undergraduate level.

## Notes on Online Courses

All students enrolled in this course have been added to the team in Microsoft Teams for the corresponding course number.
  
You can attend the online course in Microsoft Teams at the following channel:
